Old {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = Sanjama flown copper plate roof |Alternative name = Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=October 16th|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}}|Shinto ritual = {{ill|lt=Kawazu's Tori Shojin Sake Shojin|Q11553983|WD=Q11553983|lt=Tori Shojin Sake Shojin|1=Tori Shojin Sake Shojin}} ({{ill|lt=December 18th|Q2438|WD=Q2438|1=December 18}} - {{ill|lt=December 23rd|Q2676|WD=Q2676|lt=23rd|1=December 23}}) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|lat_deg=34|lat_min=45|lat_sec=15.3|lon_deg=138|lon_min=59|lon_sec=20.6|label=Shinto Torii
Shrine|position=left|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} }} [[File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, otorii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Otorii}}]]'''Sugihokowake no Mikoto Shrine'' is located in {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Tanaka. In {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. '''Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine'' (not ``Kawazu'') [https://sugihokowakenomikoto.jimdo.com/ Official site of Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine (Sugisawabetsu no Mikoto Shrine)][http://www.kawazu-onsen.com/sightseeing/25 Large camphor tree of Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine] - Kawazu Town Tourism Association. It is in charge of '''{{ill|lt=#Himemiya Shrine|UNKNOWN|lt=Himemiya Shrine}}''', which is compared to ``Sasahara Hime no Mikoto Shrine'', a Shikinai shrine. === Company name === In ancient times, it was called ``Sawabetsu Myojin'', ``Kino (Great) Myojin'', ``Kinomiya (Great) Myojin'', ``Kino Shrine'', ``Kino Shrine'', and ``Kinosaki Myojin'', and even today it is commonly known as ``Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine''. It is said to be a shrine with {{ill|lt=Kinomiya faith|Q11296898|WD=Q11296898|1=Kinomiya Faith}}, as it has passed down the ancient name of ``Kinomiya'' and ``Tori Shojin Sake Shojin'' (described later). === Enshrined God === The main deity is ``Sugihokowake no Mikoto'', and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Isotake no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukuhikona no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} are enshrined in {{ill|lt=Aidono|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}}. In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}", it is listed as 1 dai, so it is thought that it was originally dedicated to Sugisubetsu no Mikoto. . === History ===According to the shrine's legend, it had been enshrined since ancient times, but was rebuilt in the {{ill|lt=Wado|Q1373026|WD=Q1373026|1=Wadō (era)}} period (708 - 15), and was listed as a small shrine in the Engishiki Shinmyocho. In addition, in the shrine biography, in {{ill|lt=Kenkyu|Q1193933|WD=Q1193933|1=Kenkyū}}4 ({{ill|lt=1193|Q19741|WD=Q19741|1=1193}}), {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800|WD=Q242800|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} rebuilt it and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sharyo|1=UNKNOWN}}93{{ill|lt=stone (Unit)|Q855048|WD=Q855048|lt=stone|1=Koku}} was dedicated, and the shrine building was further rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Fujiwara Yoritsune|Q471780|WD=Q471780|1=Kujō Yoritsune}} in {{ill|lt=1226|Q5405|WD=Q5405|1=1226}}. In {{ill|lt=Yasunaga|Q980266|WD=Q980266|1=Kōei}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1343|Q6072|WD=Q6072|1=1343}}), {{ill|lt=Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}}, ``{{ill|lt=Izu Province Shinto Name Book|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu Province Shinto Name Book}},'' it is written as ``Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Hokowake no Myojin.'' According to the shrine's legend, the shrine was rebuilt by Yoshitane Ashikaga|Q708527} in {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}}10 ({{ill|lt=1513|Q6200|WD=Q6200|1=1513}}), and 12 families served {{ill|lt=Shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367|1=Shake (social class)}}. (era name)|Q1192853|Tenbun}} In the 7th year ({{ill|lt=1538|Q6470|WD=Q6470|1=1538}}), the shrine and other buildings were completely destroyed by fire, and in the 13th year of the same year ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=1544|1=UNKNOWN}}), after the shrine's territory was washed away by a flood, it fell into disrepair and was enshrined in a small shrine, but it is said that the governor of the country {{ill|lt=Yasuhide Shimizu|Q11560955|WD=Q11560955|1=Shimizu Yasuhide}} and others rebuilt it.In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), he was appointed to Gosha. {{ill|lt=Postwar|Q1201472|WD=Q1201472|1=Post-war}} belongs to {{ill|lt=Shrine Headquarters|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. Furthermore, the Shinto priest has been served by the {{ill|lt=Kawazu clan|Q11553993|WD=Q11553993|1=Kawazu clan}} for generations. === Precincts === === Shrine building === The main shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Kasho|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157|1=Kashō}}2 ({{ill|lt=1819|Q6967|WD=Q6967|1=1819}}), and is {{ill|lt=Nagarezukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}. The roof is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=copper plate roof|1=UNKNOWN}} with {{ill|lt=gable #gable style|Q4881511|WD=Q4881511|lt=staggered gable|1=Bargeboard}} and eaves {{ill|lt=gable #gable style|Q4881511|WD=Q4881511|lt=kara gable|1=Bargeboard}}. File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, honden.jpg|Main shrine File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, haiden.jpg|House of worship File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, torii.jpg|Torii in the precincts === Big Kusu of Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine === [[File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, okusu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|{{center|[[Big Kusu of Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja|Big Kusu]] (National Natural Monument)}}]] {{Main|Big Kusu of Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine}} A {{ill|lt=camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722|lt=camphor|1=Camphora officinarum}} with a tree height of about 24m and a trunk circumference of 14m. The tree was estimated to be over 1,000 years old in the early {{ill|lt=Showa period|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} period (first half of the 20th century), and was designated as a national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}} in 1936|Q18649}}. Since ancient times, it has been called ``Kimiya-sama's great Kusu'' and has been worshiped as {{ill|lt=sacred tree|Q48743901|WD=Q48743901|1=Shinboku}}. There were once seven large camphor trees in Kawazu, and until the mid-Meiji period they were called ``Nanaka Kae Nanakusu,'' but this is the only one that remains today. There is also a large camphor tree near the approach entrance and in front of the shrine to the right. In addition, there is also {{ill|lt=Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682|WD=Q11523682|1=Kinomiya Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Atami City|Q653364|WD=Q653364|1=Atami}} in the northern part of {{ill|lt=Izu Peninsula|Q822822|WD=Q822822|1=Izu Peninsula}}. alink|Azu Sawaki Shrine's Large Kusu|Q60403026}} There is a famous camphor tree (also a natural monument), which is often called the same name as "Kimiya Shrine's Kusu", so it is easy to confuse it. File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja 20100601 (3).jpg|Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja ShrineFile:Cinnamomum camphora 20100601 (Kawazu) (B).jpg|Cinnamomum camphora at the entrance to the approach === Setsusha === Doso Shrine, Uga Shrine, Inari Shrine, Tenjin Shrine, Mizu Shrine, Kumano Shrine, Kojima Shrine, Toshino Shrine, Hobutsuki Shrine, Ohgusu Shrine, Sa Shrine, Yama Shrine, Onagotsusa Shrine File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, keidaisha.jpg|Precinct shrine === Festival === Sakeru Festival (December 17th) / Sake Festival (December 24th) - Between the two festivals (December 18th - 23rd) is called {{ill|lt=Kawazu's Tori Shojin Sake Shojin|Q11553983|WD=Q11553983|1=Kawazu's Tori Shojin Sake Shojin}}, and it is said that when Sugisabetsu no Mikoto, who was drunk in the area, was about to burn to death in a wild fire, he was saved thanks to a large flock of small birds carrying water from their wings and extinguishing the fire, so Sugisabetsu no Mikoto forbade him to drink alcohol and eat poultry and eggs. === Himemiya Shrine === {{Shrine |Name = Himemiya Shrine |Image = [[File:Himemiya-jinja (Kawazu), shaden-1.jpg|280px]]
Shrine building
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|34|45|06.1|N|138|59|34.3|E}}}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kamo District|Q961511|WD=Q961511|1=Kamo District, Shizuoka}}{{ill|lt=Kawazu Town|Q1203861|WD=Q1203861|1=Kawazu, Shizuoka}}Sasahara 314 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 steps degrees minutes seconds|34|45|06.1|N|138|59|34.3|E|scale:25000|name=Himemiya Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = Sasahara Hime no Mikoto |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} (small)
Old {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Soken = |Main shrine = Ikkensha-ryu-zukuri thatched roof |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = |Shinto ritual = }} === Company name === In ancient times, it was called ``Himemiya'' or ``Himemiya Daimyojin.'' === Enshrined God === Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine ({{jalink|Emperor Nintoku|Q3 13119|Osazaki no Mikoto) and Kageyama Shrine (Kageyama Myojin) were enshrined togetherProperly, it was enshrined at Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, and the main deity was changed to Sasahara Hime no Mikoto. . === History === Ritual artifacts thought to date back to the 4th century have been unearthed near the former shrine grounds (305 Sasahara Himamiya, Kawazu-machi, currently on the grounds of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kawazu Town Minami Elementary School|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himemiya Kindergarten|1=UNKNOWN}}) ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himemiya Ruins|1=UNKNOWN}}), and it appears that it has been worshiped as a {{ill|lt=Tsubojin|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} since ancient times. It is listed as a small shrine in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho'', and is described as ``Junior Fourth Rank Upper Sawarahime no Miko'' in the ``Izu Province Shinto Namecho''. According to the shrine's biography, the shrine had territory until around {{ill|lt=Motogame|Q1145064|WD=Q1145064|1=Genki (era)}} (1570 - 73), but for the {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} years (1573 - It is said that it was confiscated in 1992), and in {{ill|lt=Bunroku|Q1373039|WD=Q1373039|1=Bunroku}}5 ({{ill|lt=1596|Q6785|WD=Q6785|1=1596}}), a company official called Tosho took away the old records and declined. It was rebuilt by the people of the entire Kawazu village in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 4th year ({{ill|lt=1664|Q7581|WD=Q7581|1=1664}}), and was listed in {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} in 1896, In 1213, Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, which was located at the current site (slightly northeast of the current site), was enshrined together with the nearby Kageyama Shrine, and the company name was changed to ``Himemiya Shrine'' and the main deity was changed to ``Sasahara Hime no Mikoto''. - It was rebuilt in 2019). , In 1962, it was moved to the side of the main shrine of Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine, but it was moved again in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}}14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987|1=2002}}). === Shrine building === The main shrine is one-span {{ill|lt=nagaru-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} on both the front and sides, with a {{ill|lt=杮thatched|Q199388|WD=Q199388|1=Wood shingle}} roof with a staggered gable and a kara gable on the eaves. It is a late {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} shrine building. File:Himemiya-jinja (Kawazu), kyushati.jpg|Kawazu Town Minami Elementary School (Himemiya Ruins) === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === References === *Shikinaisha Research Report, edited by Shikinaisha Research Association, Volume 10, Izu Province, Kai Province, Kogakkan University Publishing Department, 1981. *Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites, edited by Kenichi Tanigawa, Volume 10, Tokai, Hakusuisha, 1987 ISBN 4-560-02220-8 === Transportation === *Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine is a 15-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Izukyu Line|Q864265|WD=Q864265|1=Izu Kyūkō Line}}{{ill|lt=Kawazu Station|Q6379913|WD=Q6379913|1=Kawazu Station}}. Along the way is the entrance to Himemiya Shrine. === External link === {{Commonscat|Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja}} {{Commonscat|Himemiya-jinja (Kawazu)|Himemiya Shrine}} *[https://sugihokowakenomikoto.jimdoweb.com/ Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine (Sugisawa Betsu no Mikoto Shrine)] - Official website * {{Twitter|kawazukinomiya|Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine}} * {{Instagram|kawazukinomiyashrine|Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine (Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine)}} {{Shinto horizontal}} {{Redirect belongs to category |redirect1=Himemiya Shrine (Kawazu Town) |1-1=Shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture |1-2=Shikinaisha in Izunokuni |1-3=Old village shrine in Shizuoka prefecture |1-4=Buildings in Kawazu Town |1-5=History of Kawazu Town }} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Sugihokowake no Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Sugihokowake no Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧郷社]] [[Category:河津町の建築物]] [[Category:河津町の歴史]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

Old {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = Sanjama flown copper plate roof |Alternative name = Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=October 16th|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}}|Shinto ritual = {{ill|lt=Kawazu's Tori Shojin Sake Shojin|Q11553983|WD=Q11553983|lt=Tori Shojin Sake Shojin|1=Tori Shojin Sake Shojin}} ({{ill|lt=December 18th|Q2438|WD=Q2438|1=December 18}} - {{ill|lt=December 23rd|Q2676|WD=Q2676|lt=23rd|1=December 23}}) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|lat_deg=34|lat_min=45|lat_sec=15.3|lon_deg=138|lon_min=59|lon_sec=20.6|label=Shinto Torii
Shrine|position=left|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} }} [[File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, otorii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Otorii}}]]'''Sugihokowake no Mikoto Shrine'' is located in {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Tanaka. In {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. '''Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine'' (not ``Kawazu'') [https://sugihokowakenomikoto.jimdo.com/ Official site of Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine (Sugisawabetsu no Mikoto Shrine)][http://www.kawazu-onsen.com/sightseeing/25 Large camphor tree of Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine] - Kawazu Town Tourism Association. It is in charge of '''{{ill|lt=#Himemiya Shrine|UNKNOWN|lt=Himemiya Shrine}}''', which is compared to ``Sasahara Hime no Mikoto Shrine'', a Shikinai shrine. === Company name === In ancient times, it was called ``Sawabetsu Myojin'', ``Kino (Great) Myojin'', ``Kinomiya (Great) Myojin'', ``Kino Shrine'', ``Kino Shrine'', and ``Kinosaki Myojin'', and even today it is commonly known as ``Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine''. It is said to be a shrine with {{ill|lt=Kinomiya faith|Q11296898|WD=Q11296898|1=Kinomiya Faith}}, as it has passed down the ancient name of ``Kinomiya'' and ``Tori Shojin Sake Shojin'' (described later). === Enshrined God === The main deity is ``Sugihokowake no Mikoto'', and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Isotake no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukuhikona no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} are enshrined in {{ill|lt=Aidono|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}}. In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}", it is listed as 1 dai, so it is thought that it was originally dedicated to Sugisubetsu no Mikoto. . === History ===According to the shrine's legend, it had been enshrined since ancient times, but was rebuilt in the {{ill|lt=Wado|Q1373026|WD=Q1373026|1=Wadō (era)}} period (708 - 15), and was listed as a small shrine in the Engishiki Shinmyocho. In addition, in the shrine biography, in {{ill|lt=Kenkyu|Q1193933|WD=Q1193933|1=Kenkyū}}4 ({{ill|lt=1193|Q19741|WD=Q19741|1=1193}}), {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800|WD=Q242800|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} rebuilt it and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sharyo|1=UNKNOWN}}93{{ill|lt=stone (Unit)|Q855048|WD=Q855048|lt=stone|1=Koku}} was dedicated, and the shrine building was further rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Fujiwara Yoritsune|Q471780|WD=Q471780|1=Kujō Yoritsune}} in {{ill|lt=1226|Q5405|WD=Q5405|1=1226}}. In {{ill|lt=Yasunaga|Q980266|WD=Q980266|1=Kōei}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1343|Q6072|WD=Q6072|1=1343}}), {{ill|lt=Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}}, ``{{ill|lt=Izu Province Shinto Name Book|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu Province Shinto Name Book}},'' it is written as ``Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Hokowake no Myojin.'' According to the shrine's legend, the shrine was rebuilt by Yoshitane Ashikaga|Q708527} in {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}}10 ({{ill|lt=1513|Q6200|WD=Q6200|1=1513}}), and 12 families served {{ill|lt=Shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367|1=Shake (social class)}}. (era name)|Q1192853|Tenbun}} In the 7th year ({{ill|lt=1538|Q6470|WD=Q6470|1=1538}}), the shrine and other buildings were completely destroyed by fire, and in the 13th year of the same year ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=1544|1=UNKNOWN}}), after the shrine's territory was washed away by a flood, it fell into disrepair and was enshrined in a small shrine, but it is said that the governor of the country {{ill|lt=Yasuhide Shimizu|Q11560955|WD=Q11560955|1=Shimizu Yasuhide}} and others rebuilt it.In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), he was appointed to Gosha. {{ill|lt=Postwar|Q1201472|WD=Q1201472|1=Post-war}} belongs to {{ill|lt=Shrine Headquarters|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. Furthermore, the Shinto priest has been served by the {{ill|lt=Kawazu clan|Q11553993|WD=Q11553993|1=Kawazu clan}} for generations. === Precincts === === Shrine building === The main shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Kasho|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157|1=Kashō}}2 ({{ill|lt=1819|Q6967|WD=Q6967|1=1819}}), and is {{ill|lt=Nagarezukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}. The roof is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=copper plate roof|1=UNKNOWN}} with {{ill|lt=gable #gable style|Q4881511|WD=Q4881511|lt=staggered gable|1=Bargeboard}} and eaves {{ill|lt=gable #gable style|Q4881511|WD=Q4881511|lt=kara gable|1=Bargeboard}}. File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, honden.jpg|Main shrine File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, haiden.jpg|House of worship File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, torii.jpg|Torii in the precincts === Big Kusu of Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine === [[File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, okusu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|{{center|[[Big Kusu of Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja|Big Kusu]] (National Natural Monument)}}]] {{Main|Big Kusu of Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine}} A {{ill|lt=camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722|lt=camphor|1=Camphora officinarum}} with a tree height of about 24m and a trunk circumference of 14m. The tree was estimated to be over 1,000 years old in the early {{ill|lt=Showa period|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} period (first half of the 20th century), and was designated as a national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}} in 1936|Q18649}}. Since ancient times, it has been called ``Kimiya-sama's great Kusu'' and has been worshiped as {{ill|lt=sacred tree|Q48743901|WD=Q48743901|1=Shinboku}}. There were once seven large camphor trees in Kawazu, and until the mid-Meiji period they were called ``Nanaka Kae Nanakusu,'' but this is the only one that remains today. There is also a large camphor tree near the approach entrance and in front of the shrine to the right. In addition, there is also {{ill|lt=Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682|WD=Q11523682|1=Kinomiya Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Atami City|Q653364|WD=Q653364|1=Atami}} in the northern part of {{ill|lt=Izu Peninsula|Q822822|WD=Q822822|1=Izu Peninsula}}. alink|Azu Sawaki Shrine's Large Kusu|Q60403026}} There is a famous camphor tree (also a natural monument), which is often called the same name as "Kimiya Shrine's Kusu", so it is easy to confuse it. File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja 20100601 (3).jpg|Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja ShrineFile:Cinnamomum camphora 20100601 (Kawazu) (B).jpg|Cinnamomum camphora at the entrance to the approach === Setsusha === Doso Shrine, Uga Shrine, Inari Shrine, Tenjin Shrine, Mizu Shrine, Kumano Shrine, Kojima Shrine, Toshino Shrine, Hobutsuki Shrine, Ohgusu Shrine, Sa Shrine, Yama Shrine, Onagotsusa Shrine File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, keidaisha.jpg|Precinct shrine === Festival === Sakeru Festival (December 17th) / Sake Festival (December 24th) - Between the two festivals (December 18th - 23rd) is called {{ill|lt=Kawazu's Tori Shojin Sake Shojin|Q11553983|WD=Q11553983|1=Kawazu's Tori Shojin Sake Shojin}}, and it is said that when Sugisabetsu no Mikoto, who was drunk in the area, was about to burn to death in a wild fire, he was saved thanks to a large flock of small birds carrying water from their wings and extinguishing the fire, so Sugisabetsu no Mikoto forbade him to drink alcohol and eat poultry and eggs. === Himemiya Shrine === {{Shrine |Name = Himemiya Shrine |Image = [[File:Himemiya-jinja (Kawazu), shaden-1.jpg|280px]]
Shrine building
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|34|45|06.1|N|138|59|34.3|E}}}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kamo District|Q961511|WD=Q961511|1=Kamo District, Shizuoka}}{{ill|lt=Kawazu Town|Q1203861|WD=Q1203861|1=Kawazu, Shizuoka}}Sasahara 314 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 steps degrees minutes seconds|34|45|06.1|N|138|59|34.3|E|scale:25000|name=Himemiya Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = Sasahara Hime no Mikoto |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} (small)
Old {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Soken = |Main shrine = Ikkensha-ryu-zukuri thatched roof |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = |Shinto ritual = }} === Company name === In ancient times, it was called ``Himemiya'' or ``Himemiya Daimyojin.'' === Enshrined God === Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine ({{jalink|Emperor Nintoku|Q3 13119|Osazaki no Mikoto) and Kageyama Shrine (Kageyama Myojin) were enshrined togetherProperly, it was enshrined at Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, and the main deity was changed to Sasahara Hime no Mikoto. . === History === Ritual artifacts thought to date back to the 4th century have been unearthed near the former shrine grounds (305 Sasahara Himamiya, Kawazu-machi, currently on the grounds of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kawazu Town Minami Elementary School|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himemiya Kindergarten|1=UNKNOWN}}) ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himemiya Ruins|1=UNKNOWN}}), and it appears that it has been worshiped as a {{ill|lt=Tsubojin|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} since ancient times. It is listed as a small shrine in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho'', and is described as ``Junior Fourth Rank Upper Sawarahime no Miko'' in the ``Izu Province Shinto Namecho''. According to the shrine's biography, the shrine had territory until around {{ill|lt=Motogame|Q1145064|WD=Q1145064|1=Genki (era)}} (1570 - 73), but for the {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} years (1573 - It is said that it was confiscated in 1992), and in {{ill|lt=Bunroku|Q1373039|WD=Q1373039|1=Bunroku}}5 ({{ill|lt=1596|Q6785|WD=Q6785|1=1596}}), a company official called Tosho took away the old records and declined. It was rebuilt by the people of the entire Kawazu village in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 4th year ({{ill|lt=1664|Q7581|WD=Q7581|1=1664}}), and was listed in {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} in 1896, In 1213, Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, which was located at the current site (slightly northeast of the current site), was enshrined together with the nearby Kageyama Shrine, and the company name was changed to ``Himemiya Shrine'' and the main deity was changed to ``Sasahara Hime no Mikoto''. - It was rebuilt in 2019). , In 1962, it was moved to the side of the main shrine of Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine, but it was moved again in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}}14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987|1=2002}}). === Shrine building === The main shrine is one-span {{ill|lt=nagaru-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} on both the front and sides, with a {{ill|lt=杮thatched|Q199388|WD=Q199388|1=Wood shingle}} roof with a staggered gable and a kara gable on the eaves. It is a late {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} shrine building. File:Himemiya-jinja (Kawazu), kyushati.jpg|Kawazu Town Minami Elementary School (Himemiya Ruins) === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === References === *Shikinaisha Research Report, edited by Shikinaisha Research Association, Volume 10, Izu Province, Kai Province, Kogakkan University Publishing Department, 1981. *Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites, edited by Kenichi Tanigawa, Volume 10, Tokai, Hakusuisha, 1987 ISBN 4-560-02220-8 === Transportation === *Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine is a 15-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Izukyu Line|Q864265|WD=Q864265|1=Izu Kyūkō Line}}{{ill|lt=Kawazu Station|Q6379913|WD=Q6379913|1=Kawazu Station}}. Along the way is the entrance to Himemiya Shrine. === External link === {{Commonscat|Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja}} {{Commonscat|Himemiya-jinja (Kawazu)|Himemiya Shrine}} *[https://sugihokowakenomikoto.jimdoweb.com/ Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine (Sugisawa Betsu no Mikoto Shrine)] - Official website * {{Twitter|kawazukinomiya|Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine}} * {{Instagram|kawazukinomiyashrine|Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine (Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine)}} {{Shinto horizontal}} {{Redirect belongs to category |redirect1=Himemiya Shrine (Kawazu Town) |1-1=Shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture |1-2=Shikinaisha in Izunokuni |1-3=Old village shrine in Shizuoka prefecture |1-4=Buildings in Kawazu Town |1-5=History of Kawazu Town }} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Sugihokowake no Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Sugihokowake no Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:静岡県の旧郷社]] [[Category:河津町の建築物]] [[Category:河津町の歴史]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

Old {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = Sanjama flown copper plate roof |Alternative name = Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=October 16th|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}}|Shinto ritual = {{ill|lt=Kawazu's Tori Shojin Sake Shojin|Q11553983|WD=Q11553983|lt=Tori Shojin Sake Shojin|1=Tori Shojin Sake Shojin}} ({{ill|lt=December 18th|Q2438|WD=Q2438|1=December 18}} - {{ill|lt=December 23rd|Q2676|WD=Q2676|lt=23rd|1=December 23}}) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|lat_deg=34|lat_min=45|lat_sec=15.3|lon_deg=138|lon_min=59|lon_sec=20.6|label=Shinto Torii
Shrine|position=left|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} }} [[File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, otorii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Otorii}}]]'''Sugihokowake no Mikoto Shrine'' is located in {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Tanaka. In {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. '''Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine'' (not ``Kawazu'') [https://sugihokowakenomikoto.jimdo.com/ Official site of Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine (Sugisawabetsu no Mikoto Shrine)][http://www.kawazu-onsen.com/sightseeing/25 Large camphor tree of Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine] - Kawazu Town Tourism Association. It is in charge of '''{{ill|lt=#Himemiya Shrine|UNKNOWN|lt=Himemiya Shrine}}''', which is compared to ``Sasahara Hime no Mikoto Shrine'', a Shikinai shrine. === Company name === In ancient times, it was called ``Sawabetsu Myojin'', ``Kino (Great) Myojin'', ``Kinomiya (Great) Myojin'', ``Kino Shrine'', ``Kino Shrine'', and ``Kinosaki Myojin'', and even today it is commonly known as ``Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine''. It is said to be a shrine with {{ill|lt=Kinomiya faith|Q11296898|WD=Q11296898|1=Kinomiya Faith}}, as it has passed down the ancient name of ``Kinomiya'' and ``Tori Shojin Sake Shojin'' (described later). === Enshrined God === The main deity is ``Sugihokowake no Mikoto'', and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Isotake no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukuhikona no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} are enshrined in {{ill|lt=Aidono|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}}. In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}", it is listed as 1 dai, so it is thought that it was originally dedicated to Sugisubetsu no Mikoto. . === History ===According to the shrine's legend, it had been enshrined since ancient times, but was rebuilt in the {{ill|lt=Wado|Q1373026|WD=Q1373026|1=Wadō (era)}} period (708 - 15), and was listed as a small shrine in the Engishiki Shinmyocho. In addition, in the shrine biography, in {{ill|lt=Kenkyu|Q1193933|WD=Q1193933|1=Kenkyū}}4 ({{ill|lt=1193|Q19741|WD=Q19741|1=1193}}), {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800|WD=Q242800|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} rebuilt it and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sharyo|1=UNKNOWN}}93{{ill|lt=stone (Unit)|Q855048|WD=Q855048|lt=stone|1=Koku}} was dedicated, and the shrine building was further rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Fujiwara Yoritsune|Q471780|WD=Q471780|1=Kujō Yoritsune}} in {{ill|lt=1226|Q5405|WD=Q5405|1=1226}}. In {{ill|lt=Yasunaga|Q980266|WD=Q980266|1=Kōei}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1343|Q6072|WD=Q6072|1=1343}}), {{ill|lt=Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}}, ``{{ill|lt=Izu Province Shinto Name Book|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu Province Shinto Name Book}},'' it is written as ``Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Hokowake no Myojin.'' According to the shrine's legend, the shrine was rebuilt by Yoshitane Ashikaga|Q708527} in {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}}10 ({{ill|lt=1513|Q6200|WD=Q6200|1=1513}}), and 12 families served {{ill|lt=Shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367|1=Shake (social class)}}. (era name)|Q1192853|Tenbun}} In the 7th year ({{ill|lt=1538|Q6470|WD=Q6470|1=1538}}), the shrine and other buildings were completely destroyed by fire, and in the 13th year of the same year ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=1544|1=UNKNOWN}}), after the shrine's territory was washed away by a flood, it fell into disrepair and was enshrined in a small shrine, but it is said that the governor of the country {{ill|lt=Yasuhide Shimizu|Q11560955|WD=Q11560955|1=Shimizu Yasuhide}} and others rebuilt it.In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), he was appointed to Gosha. {{ill|lt=Postwar|Q1201472|WD=Q1201472|1=Post-war}} belongs to {{ill|lt=Shrine Headquarters|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. Furthermore, the Shinto priest has been served by the {{ill|lt=Kawazu clan|Q11553993|WD=Q11553993|1=Kawazu clan}} for generations. === Precincts === === Shrine building === The main shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Kasho|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157|1=Kashō}}2 ({{ill|lt=1819|Q6967|WD=Q6967|1=1819}}), and is {{ill|lt=Nagarezukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}. The roof is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=copper plate roof|1=UNKNOWN}} with {{ill|lt=gable #gable style|Q4881511|WD=Q4881511|lt=staggered gable|1=Bargeboard}} and eaves {{ill|lt=gable #gable style|Q4881511|WD=Q4881511|lt=kara gable|1=Bargeboard}}. File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, honden.jpg|Main shrine File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, haiden.jpg|House of worship File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, torii.jpg|Torii in the precincts === Big Kusu of Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine === [[File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, okusu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|{{center|[[Big Kusu of Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja|Big Kusu]] (National Natural Monument)}}]] {{Main|Big Kusu of Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine}} A {{ill|lt=camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722|lt=camphor|1=Camphora officinarum}} with a tree height of about 24m and a trunk circumference of 14m. The tree was estimated to be over 1,000 years old in the early {{ill|lt=Showa period|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} period (first half of the 20th century), and was designated as a national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}} in 1936|Q18649}}. Since ancient times, it has been called ``Kimiya-sama's great Kusu'' and has been worshiped as {{ill|lt=sacred tree|Q48743901|WD=Q48743901|1=Shinboku}}. There were once seven large camphor trees in Kawazu, and until the mid-Meiji period they were called ``Nanaka Kae Nanakusu,'' but this is the only one that remains today. There is also a large camphor tree near the approach entrance and in front of the shrine to the right. In addition, there is also {{ill|lt=Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682|WD=Q11523682|1=Kinomiya Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Atami City|Q653364|WD=Q653364|1=Atami}} in the northern part of {{ill|lt=Izu Peninsula|Q822822|WD=Q822822|1=Izu Peninsula}}. alink|Azu Sawaki Shrine's Large Kusu|Q60403026}} There is a famous camphor tree (also a natural monument), which is often called the same name as "Kimiya Shrine's Kusu", so it is easy to confuse it. File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja 20100601 (3).jpg|Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja ShrineFile:Cinnamomum camphora 20100601 (Kawazu) (B).jpg|Cinnamomum camphora at the entrance to the approach === Setsusha === Doso Shrine, Uga Shrine, Inari Shrine, Tenjin Shrine, Mizu Shrine, Kumano Shrine, Kojima Shrine, Toshino Shrine, Hobutsuki Shrine, Ohgusu Shrine, Sa Shrine, Yama Shrine, Onagotsusa Shrine File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, keidaisha.jpg|Precinct shrine === Festival === Sakeru Festival (December 17th) / Sake Festival (December 24th) - Between the two festivals (December 18th - 23rd) is called {{ill|lt=Kawazu's Tori Shojin Sake Shojin|Q11553983|WD=Q11553983|1=Kawazu's Tori Shojin Sake Shojin}}, and it is said that when Sugisabetsu no Mikoto, who was drunk in the area, was about to burn to death in a wild fire, he was saved thanks to a large flock of small birds carrying water from their wings and extinguishing the fire, so Sugisabetsu no Mikoto forbade him to drink alcohol and eat poultry and eggs. === Himemiya Shrine === {{Shrine |Name = Himemiya Shrine |Image = [[File:Himemiya-jinja (Kawazu), shaden-1.jpg|280px]]
Shrine building
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|34|45|06.1|N|138|59|34.3|E}}}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kamo District|Q961511|WD=Q961511|1=Kamo District, Shizuoka}}{{ill|lt=Kawazu Town|Q1203861|WD=Q1203861|1=Kawazu, Shizuoka}}Sasahara 314 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 steps degrees minutes seconds|34|45|06.1|N|138|59|34.3|E|scale:25000|name=Himemiya Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = Sasahara Hime no Mikoto |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} (small)
Old {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Soken = |Main shrine = Ikkensha-ryu-zukuri thatched roof |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = |Shinto ritual = }} === Company name === In ancient times, it was called ``Himemiya'' or ``Himemiya Daimyojin.'' === Enshrined God === Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine ({{jalink|Emperor Nintoku|Q3 13119|Osazaki no Mikoto) and Kageyama Shrine (Kageyama Myojin) were enshrined togetherProperly, it was enshrined at Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, and the main deity was changed to Sasahara Hime no Mikoto. . === History === Ritual artifacts thought to date back to the 4th century have been unearthed near the former shrine grounds (305 Sasahara Himamiya, Kawazu-machi, currently on the grounds of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kawazu Town Minami Elementary School|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himemiya Kindergarten|1=UNKNOWN}}) ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himemiya Ruins|1=UNKNOWN}}), and it appears that it has been worshiped as a {{ill|lt=Tsubojin|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} since ancient times. It is listed as a small shrine in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho'', and is described as ``Junior Fourth Rank Upper Sawarahime no Miko'' in the ``Izu Province Shinto Namecho''. According to the shrine's biography, the shrine had territory until around {{ill|lt=Motogame|Q1145064|WD=Q1145064|1=Genki (era)}} (1570 - 73), but for the {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} years (1573 - It is said that it was confiscated in 1992), and in {{ill|lt=Bunroku|Q1373039|WD=Q1373039|1=Bunroku}}5 ({{ill|lt=1596|Q6785|WD=Q6785|1=1596}}), a company official called Tosho took away the old records and declined. It was rebuilt by the people of the entire Kawazu village in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 4th year ({{ill|lt=1664|Q7581|WD=Q7581|1=1664}}), and was listed in {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} in 1896, In 1213, Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, which was located at the current site (slightly northeast of the current site), was enshrined together with the nearby Kageyama Shrine, and the company name was changed to ``Himemiya Shrine'' and the main deity was changed to ``Sasahara Hime no Mikoto''. - It was rebuilt in 2019). , In 1962, it was moved to the side of the main shrine of Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine, but it was moved again in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}}14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987|1=2002}}). === Shrine building === The main shrine is one-span {{ill|lt=nagaru-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} on both the front and sides, with a {{ill|lt=杮thatched|Q199388|WD=Q199388|1=Wood shingle}} roof with a staggered gable and a kara gable on the eaves. It is a late {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} shrine building. File:Himemiya-jinja (Kawazu), kyushati.jpg|Kawazu Town Minami Elementary School (Himemiya Ruins) === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === References === *Shikinaisha Research Report, edited by Shikinaisha Research Association, Volume 10, Izu Province, Kai Province, Kogakkan University Publishing Department, 1981. *Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites, edited by Kenichi Tanigawa, Volume 10, Tokai, Hakusuisha, 1987 ISBN 4-560-02220-8 === Transportation === *Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine is a 15-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Izukyu Line|Q864265|WD=Q864265|1=Izu Kyūkō Line}}{{ill|lt=Kawazu Station|Q6379913|WD=Q6379913|1=Kawazu Station}}. Along the way is the entrance to Himemiya Shrine. === External link === {{Commonscat|Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja}} {{Commonscat|Himemiya-jinja (Kawazu)|Himemiya Shrine}} *[https://sugihokowakenomikoto.jimdoweb.com/ Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine (Sugisawa Betsu no Mikoto Shrine)] - Official website * {{Twitter|kawazukinomiya|Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine}} * {{Instagram|kawazukinomiyashrine|Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine (Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine)}} {{Shinto horizontal}} {{Redirect belongs to category |redirect1=Himemiya Shrine (Kawazu Town) |1-1=Shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture |1-2=Shikinaisha in Izunokuni |1-3=Old village shrine in Shizuoka prefecture |1-4=Buildings in Kawazu Town |1-5=History of Kawazu Town }} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Sugihokowake no Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Sugihokowake no Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:河津町の建築物]] [[Category:河津町の歴史]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

Old {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = Sanjama flown copper plate roof |Alternative name = Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=October 16th|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}}|Shinto ritual = {{ill|lt=Kawazu's Tori Shojin Sake Shojin|Q11553983|WD=Q11553983|lt=Tori Shojin Sake Shojin|1=Tori Shojin Sake Shojin}} ({{ill|lt=December 18th|Q2438|WD=Q2438|1=December 18}} - {{ill|lt=December 23rd|Q2676|WD=Q2676|lt=23rd|1=December 23}}) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|lat_deg=34|lat_min=45|lat_sec=15.3|lon_deg=138|lon_min=59|lon_sec=20.6|label=Shinto Torii
Shrine|position=left|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} }} [[File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, otorii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Otorii}}]]'''Sugihokowake no Mikoto Shrine'' is located in {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Tanaka. In {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. '''Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine'' (not ``Kawazu'') [https://sugihokowakenomikoto.jimdo.com/ Official site of Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine (Sugisawabetsu no Mikoto Shrine)][http://www.kawazu-onsen.com/sightseeing/25 Large camphor tree of Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine] - Kawazu Town Tourism Association. It is in charge of '''{{ill|lt=#Himemiya Shrine|UNKNOWN|lt=Himemiya Shrine}}''', which is compared to ``Sasahara Hime no Mikoto Shrine'', a Shikinai shrine. === Company name === In ancient times, it was called ``Sawabetsu Myojin'', ``Kino (Great) Myojin'', ``Kinomiya (Great) Myojin'', ``Kino Shrine'', ``Kino Shrine'', and ``Kinosaki Myojin'', and even today it is commonly known as ``Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine''. It is said to be a shrine with {{ill|lt=Kinomiya faith|Q11296898|WD=Q11296898|1=Kinomiya Faith}}, as it has passed down the ancient name of ``Kinomiya'' and ``Tori Shojin Sake Shojin'' (described later). === Enshrined God === The main deity is ``Sugihokowake no Mikoto'', and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Isotake no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukuhikona no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} are enshrined in {{ill|lt=Aidono|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}}. In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}", it is listed as 1 dai, so it is thought that it was originally dedicated to Sugisubetsu no Mikoto. . === History ===According to the shrine's legend, it had been enshrined since ancient times, but was rebuilt in the {{ill|lt=Wado|Q1373026|WD=Q1373026|1=Wadō (era)}} period (708 - 15), and was listed as a small shrine in the Engishiki Shinmyocho. In addition, in the shrine biography, in {{ill|lt=Kenkyu|Q1193933|WD=Q1193933|1=Kenkyū}}4 ({{ill|lt=1193|Q19741|WD=Q19741|1=1193}}), {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800|WD=Q242800|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} rebuilt it and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sharyo|1=UNKNOWN}}93{{ill|lt=stone (Unit)|Q855048|WD=Q855048|lt=stone|1=Koku}} was dedicated, and the shrine building was further rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Fujiwara Yoritsune|Q471780|WD=Q471780|1=Kujō Yoritsune}} in {{ill|lt=1226|Q5405|WD=Q5405|1=1226}}. In {{ill|lt=Yasunaga|Q980266|WD=Q980266|1=Kōei}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1343|Q6072|WD=Q6072|1=1343}}), {{ill|lt=Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}}, ``{{ill|lt=Izu Province Shinto Name Book|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu Province Shinto Name Book}},'' it is written as ``Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Hokowake no Myojin.'' According to the shrine's legend, the shrine was rebuilt by Yoshitane Ashikaga|Q708527} in {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}}10 ({{ill|lt=1513|Q6200|WD=Q6200|1=1513}}), and 12 families served {{ill|lt=Shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367|1=Shake (social class)}}. (era name)|Q1192853|Tenbun}} In the 7th year ({{ill|lt=1538|Q6470|WD=Q6470|1=1538}}), the shrine and other buildings were completely destroyed by fire, and in the 13th year of the same year ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=1544|1=UNKNOWN}}), after the shrine's territory was washed away by a flood, it fell into disrepair and was enshrined in a small shrine, but it is said that the governor of the country {{ill|lt=Yasuhide Shimizu|Q11560955|WD=Q11560955|1=Shimizu Yasuhide}} and others rebuilt it.In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), he was appointed to Gosha. {{ill|lt=Postwar|Q1201472|WD=Q1201472|1=Post-war}} belongs to {{ill|lt=Shrine Headquarters|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. Furthermore, the Shinto priest has been served by the {{ill|lt=Kawazu clan|Q11553993|WD=Q11553993|1=Kawazu clan}} for generations. === Precincts === === Shrine building === The main shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Kasho|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157|1=Kashō}}2 ({{ill|lt=1819|Q6967|WD=Q6967|1=1819}}), and is {{ill|lt=Nagarezukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}. The roof is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=copper plate roof|1=UNKNOWN}} with {{ill|lt=gable #gable style|Q4881511|WD=Q4881511|lt=staggered gable|1=Bargeboard}} and eaves {{ill|lt=gable #gable style|Q4881511|WD=Q4881511|lt=kara gable|1=Bargeboard}}. File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, honden.jpg|Main shrine File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, haiden.jpg|House of worship File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, torii.jpg|Torii in the precincts === Big Kusu of Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine === [[File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, okusu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|{{center|[[Big Kusu of Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja|Big Kusu]] (National Natural Monument)}}]] {{Main|Big Kusu of Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine}} A {{ill|lt=camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722|lt=camphor|1=Camphora officinarum}} with a tree height of about 24m and a trunk circumference of 14m. The tree was estimated to be over 1,000 years old in the early {{ill|lt=Showa period|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} period (first half of the 20th century), and was designated as a national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}} in 1936|Q18649}}. Since ancient times, it has been called ``Kimiya-sama's great Kusu'' and has been worshiped as {{ill|lt=sacred tree|Q48743901|WD=Q48743901|1=Shinboku}}. There were once seven large camphor trees in Kawazu, and until the mid-Meiji period they were called ``Nanaka Kae Nanakusu,'' but this is the only one that remains today. There is also a large camphor tree near the approach entrance and in front of the shrine to the right. In addition, there is also {{ill|lt=Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682|WD=Q11523682|1=Kinomiya Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Atami City|Q653364|WD=Q653364|1=Atami}} in the northern part of {{ill|lt=Izu Peninsula|Q822822|WD=Q822822|1=Izu Peninsula}}. alink|Azu Sawaki Shrine's Large Kusu|Q60403026}} There is a famous camphor tree (also a natural monument), which is often called the same name as "Kimiya Shrine's Kusu", so it is easy to confuse it. File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja 20100601 (3).jpg|Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja ShrineFile:Cinnamomum camphora 20100601 (Kawazu) (B).jpg|Cinnamomum camphora at the entrance to the approach === Setsusha === Doso Shrine, Uga Shrine, Inari Shrine, Tenjin Shrine, Mizu Shrine, Kumano Shrine, Kojima Shrine, Toshino Shrine, Hobutsuki Shrine, Ohgusu Shrine, Sa Shrine, Yama Shrine, Onagotsusa Shrine File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, keidaisha.jpg|Precinct shrine === Festival === Sakeru Festival (December 17th) / Sake Festival (December 24th) - Between the two festivals (December 18th - 23rd) is called {{ill|lt=Kawazu's Tori Shojin Sake Shojin|Q11553983|WD=Q11553983|1=Kawazu's Tori Shojin Sake Shojin}}, and it is said that when Sugisabetsu no Mikoto, who was drunk in the area, was about to burn to death in a wild fire, he was saved thanks to a large flock of small birds carrying water from their wings and extinguishing the fire, so Sugisabetsu no Mikoto forbade him to drink alcohol and eat poultry and eggs. === Himemiya Shrine === {{Shrine |Name = Himemiya Shrine |Image = [[File:Himemiya-jinja (Kawazu), shaden-1.jpg|280px]]
Shrine building
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|34|45|06.1|N|138|59|34.3|E}}}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kamo District|Q961511|WD=Q961511|1=Kamo District, Shizuoka}}{{ill|lt=Kawazu Town|Q1203861|WD=Q1203861|1=Kawazu, Shizuoka}}Sasahara 314 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 steps degrees minutes seconds|34|45|06.1|N|138|59|34.3|E|scale:25000|name=Himemiya Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = Sasahara Hime no Mikoto |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} (small)
Old {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Soken = |Main shrine = Ikkensha-ryu-zukuri thatched roof |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = |Shinto ritual = }} === Company name === In ancient times, it was called ``Himemiya'' or ``Himemiya Daimyojin.'' === Enshrined God === Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine ({{jalink|Emperor Nintoku|Q3 13119|Osazaki no Mikoto) and Kageyama Shrine (Kageyama Myojin) were enshrined togetherProperly, it was enshrined at Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, and the main deity was changed to Sasahara Hime no Mikoto. . === History === Ritual artifacts thought to date back to the 4th century have been unearthed near the former shrine grounds (305 Sasahara Himamiya, Kawazu-machi, currently on the grounds of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kawazu Town Minami Elementary School|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himemiya Kindergarten|1=UNKNOWN}}) ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himemiya Ruins|1=UNKNOWN}}), and it appears that it has been worshiped as a {{ill|lt=Tsubojin|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} since ancient times. It is listed as a small shrine in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho'', and is described as ``Junior Fourth Rank Upper Sawarahime no Miko'' in the ``Izu Province Shinto Namecho''. According to the shrine's biography, the shrine had territory until around {{ill|lt=Motogame|Q1145064|WD=Q1145064|1=Genki (era)}} (1570 - 73), but for the {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} years (1573 - It is said that it was confiscated in 1992), and in {{ill|lt=Bunroku|Q1373039|WD=Q1373039|1=Bunroku}}5 ({{ill|lt=1596|Q6785|WD=Q6785|1=1596}}), a company official called Tosho took away the old records and declined. It was rebuilt by the people of the entire Kawazu village in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 4th year ({{ill|lt=1664|Q7581|WD=Q7581|1=1664}}), and was listed in {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} in 1896, In 1213, Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, which was located at the current site (slightly northeast of the current site), was enshrined together with the nearby Kageyama Shrine, and the company name was changed to ``Himemiya Shrine'' and the main deity was changed to ``Sasahara Hime no Mikoto''. - It was rebuilt in 2019). , In 1962, it was moved to the side of the main shrine of Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine, but it was moved again in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}}14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987|1=2002}}). === Shrine building === The main shrine is one-span {{ill|lt=nagaru-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} on both the front and sides, with a {{ill|lt=杮thatched|Q199388|WD=Q199388|1=Wood shingle}} roof with a staggered gable and a kara gable on the eaves. It is a late {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} shrine building. File:Himemiya-jinja (Kawazu), kyushati.jpg|Kawazu Town Minami Elementary School (Himemiya Ruins) === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === References === *Shikinaisha Research Report, edited by Shikinaisha Research Association, Volume 10, Izu Province, Kai Province, Kogakkan University Publishing Department, 1981. *Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites, edited by Kenichi Tanigawa, Volume 10, Tokai, Hakusuisha, 1987 ISBN 4-560-02220-8 === Transportation === *Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine is a 15-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Izukyu Line|Q864265|WD=Q864265|1=Izu Kyūkō Line}}{{ill|lt=Kawazu Station|Q6379913|WD=Q6379913|1=Kawazu Station}}. Along the way is the entrance to Himemiya Shrine. === External link === {{Commonscat|Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja}} {{Commonscat|Himemiya-jinja (Kawazu)|Himemiya Shrine}} *[https://sugihokowakenomikoto.jimdoweb.com/ Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine (Sugisawa Betsu no Mikoto Shrine)] - Official website * {{Twitter|kawazukinomiya|Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine}} * {{Instagram|kawazukinomiyashrine|Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine (Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine)}} {{Shinto horizontal}} {{Redirect belongs to category |redirect1=Himemiya Shrine (Kawazu Town) |1-1=Shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture |1-2=Shikinaisha in Izunokuni |1-3=Old village shrine in Shizuoka prefecture |1-4=Buildings in Kawazu Town |1-5=History of Kawazu Town }} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Sugihokowake no Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Sugihokowake no Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:河津町の建築物]] [[Category:河津町の歴史]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

Old {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = Sanjama flown copper plate roof |Alternative name = Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=October 16th|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}}|Shinto ritual = {{ill|lt=Kawazu's Tori Shojin Sake Shojin|Q11553983|WD=Q11553983|lt=Tori Shojin Sake Shojin|1=Tori Shojin Sake Shojin}} ({{ill|lt=December 18th|Q2438|WD=Q2438|1=December 18}} - {{ill|lt=December 23rd|Q2676|WD=Q2676|lt=23rd|1=December 23}}) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|lat_deg=34|lat_min=45|lat_sec=15.3|lon_deg=138|lon_min=59|lon_sec=20.6|label=Shinto Torii
Shrine|position=left|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} }} [[File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, otorii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Otorii}}]]'''Sugihokowake no Mikoto Shrine'' is located in {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Tanaka. In {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. '''Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine'' (not ``Kawazu'') [https://sugihokowakenomikoto.jimdo.com/ Official site of Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine (Sugisawabetsu no Mikoto Shrine)][http://www.kawazu-onsen.com/sightseeing/25 Large camphor tree of Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine] - Kawazu Town Tourism Association. It is in charge of '''{{ill|lt=#Himemiya Shrine|UNKNOWN|lt=Himemiya Shrine}}''', which is compared to ``Sasahara Hime no Mikoto Shrine'', a Shikinai shrine. === Company name === In ancient times, it was called ``Sawabetsu Myojin'', ``Kino (Great) Myojin'', ``Kinomiya (Great) Myojin'', ``Kino Shrine'', ``Kino Shrine'', and ``Kinosaki Myojin'', and even today it is commonly known as ``Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine''. It is said to be a shrine with {{ill|lt=Kinomiya faith|Q11296898|WD=Q11296898|1=Kinomiya Faith}}, as it has passed down the ancient name of ``Kinomiya'' and ``Tori Shojin Sake Shojin'' (described later). === Enshrined God === The main deity is ``Sugihokowake no Mikoto'', and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Isotake no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukuhikona no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} are enshrined in {{ill|lt=Aidono|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}}. In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}", it is listed as 1 dai, so it is thought that it was originally dedicated to Sugisubetsu no Mikoto. . === History ===According to the shrine's legend, it had been enshrined since ancient times, but was rebuilt in the {{ill|lt=Wado|Q1373026|WD=Q1373026|1=Wadō (era)}} period (708 - 15), and was listed as a small shrine in the Engishiki Shinmyocho. In addition, in the shrine biography, in {{ill|lt=Kenkyu|Q1193933|WD=Q1193933|1=Kenkyū}}4 ({{ill|lt=1193|Q19741|WD=Q19741|1=1193}}), {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800|WD=Q242800|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} rebuilt it and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sharyo|1=UNKNOWN}}93{{ill|lt=stone (Unit)|Q855048|WD=Q855048|lt=stone|1=Koku}} was dedicated, and the shrine building was further rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Fujiwara Yoritsune|Q471780|WD=Q471780|1=Kujō Yoritsune}} in {{ill|lt=1226|Q5405|WD=Q5405|1=1226}}. In {{ill|lt=Yasunaga|Q980266|WD=Q980266|1=Kōei}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1343|Q6072|WD=Q6072|1=1343}}), {{ill|lt=Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}}, ``{{ill|lt=Izu Province Shinto Name Book|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu Province Shinto Name Book}},'' it is written as ``Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Hokowake no Myojin.'' According to the shrine's legend, the shrine was rebuilt by Yoshitane Ashikaga|Q708527} in {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}}10 ({{ill|lt=1513|Q6200|WD=Q6200|1=1513}}), and 12 families served {{ill|lt=Shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367|1=Shake (social class)}}. (era name)|Q1192853|Tenbun}} In the 7th year ({{ill|lt=1538|Q6470|WD=Q6470|1=1538}}), the shrine and other buildings were completely destroyed by fire, and in the 13th year of the same year ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=1544|1=UNKNOWN}}), after the shrine's territory was washed away by a flood, it fell into disrepair and was enshrined in a small shrine, but it is said that the governor of the country {{ill|lt=Yasuhide Shimizu|Q11560955|WD=Q11560955|1=Shimizu Yasuhide}} and others rebuilt it.In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), he was appointed to Gosha. {{ill|lt=Postwar|Q1201472|WD=Q1201472|1=Post-war}} belongs to {{ill|lt=Shrine Headquarters|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. Furthermore, the Shinto priest has been served by the {{ill|lt=Kawazu clan|Q11553993|WD=Q11553993|1=Kawazu clan}} for generations. === Precincts === === Shrine building === The main shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Kasho|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157|1=Kashō}}2 ({{ill|lt=1819|Q6967|WD=Q6967|1=1819}}), and is {{ill|lt=Nagarezukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}. The roof is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=copper plate roof|1=UNKNOWN}} with {{ill|lt=gable #gable style|Q4881511|WD=Q4881511|lt=staggered gable|1=Bargeboard}} and eaves {{ill|lt=gable #gable style|Q4881511|WD=Q4881511|lt=kara gable|1=Bargeboard}}. File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, honden.jpg|Main shrine File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, haiden.jpg|House of worship File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, torii.jpg|Torii in the precincts === Big Kusu of Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine === [[File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, okusu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|{{center|[[Big Kusu of Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja|Big Kusu]] (National Natural Monument)}}]] {{Main|Big Kusu of Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine}} A {{ill|lt=camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722|lt=camphor|1=Camphora officinarum}} with a tree height of about 24m and a trunk circumference of 14m. The tree was estimated to be over 1,000 years old in the early {{ill|lt=Showa period|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} period (first half of the 20th century), and was designated as a national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}} in 1936|Q18649}}. Since ancient times, it has been called ``Kimiya-sama's great Kusu'' and has been worshiped as {{ill|lt=sacred tree|Q48743901|WD=Q48743901|1=Shinboku}}. There were once seven large camphor trees in Kawazu, and until the mid-Meiji period they were called ``Nanaka Kae Nanakusu,'' but this is the only one that remains today. There is also a large camphor tree near the approach entrance and in front of the shrine to the right. In addition, there is also {{ill|lt=Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682|WD=Q11523682|1=Kinomiya Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Atami City|Q653364|WD=Q653364|1=Atami}} in the northern part of {{ill|lt=Izu Peninsula|Q822822|WD=Q822822|1=Izu Peninsula}}. alink|Azu Sawaki Shrine's Large Kusu|Q60403026}} There is a famous camphor tree (also a natural monument), which is often called the same name as "Kimiya Shrine's Kusu", so it is easy to confuse it. File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja 20100601 (3).jpg|Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja ShrineFile:Cinnamomum camphora 20100601 (Kawazu) (B).jpg|Cinnamomum camphora at the entrance to the approach === Setsusha === Doso Shrine, Uga Shrine, Inari Shrine, Tenjin Shrine, Mizu Shrine, Kumano Shrine, Kojima Shrine, Toshino Shrine, Hobutsuki Shrine, Ohgusu Shrine, Sa Shrine, Yama Shrine, Onagotsusa Shrine File:Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja, keidaisha.jpg|Precinct shrine === Festival === Sakeru Festival (December 17th) / Sake Festival (December 24th) - Between the two festivals (December 18th - 23rd) is called {{ill|lt=Kawazu's Tori Shojin Sake Shojin|Q11553983|WD=Q11553983|1=Kawazu's Tori Shojin Sake Shojin}}, and it is said that when Sugisabetsu no Mikoto, who was drunk in the area, was about to burn to death in a wild fire, he was saved thanks to a large flock of small birds carrying water from their wings and extinguishing the fire, so Sugisabetsu no Mikoto forbade him to drink alcohol and eat poultry and eggs. === Himemiya Shrine === {{Shrine |Name = Himemiya Shrine |Image = [[File:Himemiya-jinja (Kawazu), shaden-1.jpg|280px]]
Shrine building
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|34|45|06.1|N|138|59|34.3|E}}}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kamo District|Q961511|WD=Q961511|1=Kamo District, Shizuoka}}{{ill|lt=Kawazu Town|Q1203861|WD=Q1203861|1=Kawazu, Shizuoka}}Sasahara 314 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 steps degrees minutes seconds|34|45|06.1|N|138|59|34.3|E|scale:25000|name=Himemiya Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = Sasahara Hime no Mikoto |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} (small)
Old {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Soken = |Main shrine = Ikkensha-ryu-zukuri thatched roof |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = |Shinto ritual = }} === Company name === In ancient times, it was called ``Himemiya'' or ``Himemiya Daimyojin.'' === Enshrined God === Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine ({{jalink|Emperor Nintoku|Q3 13119|Osazaki no Mikoto) and Kageyama Shrine (Kageyama Myojin) were enshrined togetherProperly, it was enshrined at Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, and the main deity was changed to Sasahara Hime no Mikoto. . === History === Ritual artifacts thought to date back to the 4th century have been unearthed near the former shrine grounds (305 Sasahara Himamiya, Kawazu-machi, currently on the grounds of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kawazu Town Minami Elementary School|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himemiya Kindergarten|1=UNKNOWN}}) ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himemiya Ruins|1=UNKNOWN}}), and it appears that it has been worshiped as a {{ill|lt=Tsubojin|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} since ancient times. It is listed as a small shrine in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho'', and is described as ``Junior Fourth Rank Upper Sawarahime no Miko'' in the ``Izu Province Shinto Namecho''. According to the shrine's biography, the shrine had territory until around {{ill|lt=Motogame|Q1145064|WD=Q1145064|1=Genki (era)}} (1570 - 73), but for the {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} years (1573 - It is said that it was confiscated in 1992), and in {{ill|lt=Bunroku|Q1373039|WD=Q1373039|1=Bunroku}}5 ({{ill|lt=1596|Q6785|WD=Q6785|1=1596}}), a company official called Tosho took away the old records and declined. It was rebuilt by the people of the entire Kawazu village in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 4th year ({{ill|lt=1664|Q7581|WD=Q7581|1=1664}}), and was listed in {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} in 1896, In 1213, Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, which was located at the current site (slightly northeast of the current site), was enshrined together with the nearby Kageyama Shrine, and the company name was changed to ``Himemiya Shrine'' and the main deity was changed to ``Sasahara Hime no Mikoto''. - It was rebuilt in 2019). , In 1962, it was moved to the side of the main shrine of Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine, but it was moved again in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}}14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987|1=2002}}). === Shrine building === The main shrine is one-span {{ill|lt=nagaru-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} on both the front and sides, with a {{ill|lt=杮thatched|Q199388|WD=Q199388|1=Wood shingle}} roof with a staggered gable and a kara gable on the eaves. It is a late {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} shrine building. File:Himemiya-jinja (Kawazu), kyushati.jpg|Kawazu Town Minami Elementary School (Himemiya Ruins) === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === References === *Shikinaisha Research Report, edited by Shikinaisha Research Association, Volume 10, Izu Province, Kai Province, Kogakkan University Publishing Department, 1981. *Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites, edited by Kenichi Tanigawa, Volume 10, Tokai, Hakusuisha, 1987 ISBN 4-560-02220-8 === Transportation === *Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine is a 15-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Izukyu Line|Q864265|WD=Q864265|1=Izu Kyūkō Line}}{{ill|lt=Kawazu Station|Q6379913|WD=Q6379913|1=Kawazu Station}}. Along the way is the entrance to Himemiya Shrine. === External link === {{Commonscat|Sugihokowakenomikoto-jinja}} {{Commonscat|Himemiya-jinja (Kawazu)|Himemiya Shrine}} *[https://sugihokowakenomikoto.jimdoweb.com/ Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine (Sugisawa Betsu no Mikoto Shrine)] - Official website * {{Twitter|kawazukinomiya|Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine}} * {{Instagram|kawazukinomiyashrine|Kawazu Kinomiya Shrine (Sugisubetsu no Mikoto Shrine)}} {{Shinto horizontal}} {{Redirect belongs to category |redirect1=Himemiya Shrine (Kawazu Town) |1-1=Shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture |1-2=Shikinaisha in Izunokuni |1-3=Old village shrine in Shizuoka prefecture |1-4=Buildings in Kawazu Town |1-5=History of Kawazu Town }} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Sugihokowake no Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Sugihokowake no Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:河津町の建築物]] [[Category:河津町の歴史]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]
